schema.region: One of: Americas, Europe, East Asia, Southeast Asia.
schema.colMultiplier: Multiplier applied to user's USD baseline spending. 1.00 = US baseline.
schema.annualHealthcareUSD: Additive baseline annual healthcare cost in USD for a retired adult / small family. Conservative midpoint.
schema.withdrawalTaxRate: Approximate effective tax rate on retirement withdrawals for a moderate-income retiree. Heuristic — not a substitute for individualized tax modeling.
schema.swr: Suggested safe withdrawal rate baseline for this country.
schema.safetyScore: Global Peace Index score (1.0 = most peaceful, ~3.5 = least). Lower is safer.
schema.safetyRank: GPI rank out of ~163 countries. Lower is safer.
schema.confidence: high | medium | low — reflects how well-supported the parameter set is by public data.

safetySource.name: Global Peace Index (Wikipedia)
safetySource.note: GPI scores are approximate values from recent annual reports; verify against the current Wikipedia table before publishing.

countries.name: United States
countries.residencyNote: Default base case. Healthcare estimate assumes pre-Medicare ACA marketplace coverage for a retired couple.

sources.name: Trinity Study / Bengen 4% rule
IRS federal income tax brackets 2025
KFF ACA marketplace premium data

caveats: Withdrawal tax rate assumes mixed taxable/IRA/Roth portfolio at a moderate income level. Heavy pre-tax skews higher; heavy Roth skews lower.
Healthcare baseline excludes long-term care.
Safety score reflects national average; varies enormously by state and city.

countries.name: Mexico
countries.residencyNote: Temporary Resident visa (4 years renewable) requires roughly $4,300/mo income or ~$72k in savings. Permanent Resident after 4 years or with higher financial thresholds.

sources.name: Numbeo cost-of-living index (CDMX vs US average)
SAT Mexico tax authority (foreign income treatment)
Wikipedia: Visa policy of Mexico

caveats: Safety varies dramatically by state; expat retirement hubs (San Miguel de Allende, Mérida, Lake Chapala) are far safer than national average.
Mexico taxes worldwide income for residents (>183 days/yr); non-residents taxed on Mexican-source only.
Private healthcare for retirees is excellent and inexpensive; public IMSS available after residency.

countries.name: Costa Rica
countries.residencyNote: Pensionado visa: $1,000/mo lifetime pension. Rentista: $2,500/mo for 2 years or $60k deposit. Inversionista: $150k investment. No standing army since 1948.

sources.name: Numbeo cost-of-living index (San José vs US average)
Costa Rica Ministerio de Hacienda (foreign-source income)
Wikipedia: Visa policy of Costa Rica

caveats: Foreign-source income (including US retirement accounts) generally exempt from Costa Rican income tax.
Healthcare via Caja (CCSS) is mandatory for residents; supplements with private insurance common.
Most peaceful country in Central America; tourist-heavy areas have petty theft risk.

countries.name: Portugal
countries.residencyNote: D7 passive-income visa: ~$870/mo for primary applicant plus dependents. NHR (Non-Habitual Resident) tax regime previously offered 10% on foreign pensions — program revised in 2024, transitional rules apply. EU residency after 5 years.

sources.name: Numbeo cost-of-living index (Lisbon vs US average)
Portugal Autoridade Tributária (NHR program rules)
Wikipedia: Healthcare in Portugal

caveats: Lisbon and Porto have seen significant cost increases since 2020; smaller cities (Coimbra, Braga, Aveiro) closer to baseline.
NHR program changes in 2024 reduced the foreign-pension benefit for new entrants.
Among the top 10 most peaceful countries globally; very low violent crime.

countries.name: Spain
countries.residencyNote: Non-Lucrative Visa: ~$2,800/mo passive income. Path to citizenship after 10 years (or 2 for Sephardic Jews / Ibero-Americans). EU residency unlocked after 5 years.

sources.name: Numbeo cost-of-living index (Madrid vs US average)
Agencia Tributaria Spain (worldwide income for residents)
Wikipedia: Visa policy of Spain

caveats: Spain taxes worldwide income at progressive rates (19-47%). Beckham Law (24% flat on Spanish income) applies to workers, not retirees.
Wealth tax exists at the regional level; Madrid has 100% rebate, Catalonia and others do not.
Public healthcare (SNS) excellent for residents; private supplements common.

countries.name: Italy
countries.residencyNote: Elective Residence Visa: ~$3,000/mo passive income, no work permitted. 7% flat tax on all foreign income available for retirees moving to towns under 20,000 in Southern Italy (Abruzzo, Molise, Puglia, etc.) for up to 9 years.

sources.name: Numbeo cost-of-living index (Rome vs US average)
Agenzia delle Entrate Italy (7% retiree flat tax)
Wikipedia: Visa policy of Italy

caveats: The 7% flat tax requires moving to a qualifying small Southern town. Outside that, taxes are 23-43% progressive on worldwide income.
Public healthcare (SSN) is universal; quality varies significantly by region (Northern > Southern).
Tax rate shown assumes the 7% flat-tax regime.

countries.name: South Korea
countries.residencyNote: F-2 long-term residency or F-5 permanent residency pathways exist. No dedicated retirement visa; pathway typically requires prior residency, marriage, or qualifying income.

sources.name: Numbeo cost-of-living index (Seoul vs US average)
Korea National Health Insurance Service rate schedule
OECD Korea tax profile

caveats: COL varies substantially Seoul vs regional cities (±25%).
Tax treatment of foreign retirement accounts and Roth distributions is jurisdiction-specific; consult a cross-border tax specialist.
Visa path is the practical constraint for most US citizens, not the math.

countries.name: Japan
countries.residencyNote: No dedicated retirement visa. Long-term resident or spouse-of-Japanese visa are common pathways. Japan Highly Skilled Professional visa accelerates permanent residency for qualifying workers.

sources.name: Numbeo cost-of-living index (Tokyo vs US average)
Japan National Health Insurance premium schedule
OECD Japan tax profile

caveats: Japan taxes worldwide income for residents after 5 of last 10 years in country. Pre-residency window has lighter treatment.
Tokyo COL is meaningfully higher than regional Japan.
Inheritance tax exposure for long-term residents is significant — not modeled here.

countries.name: Taiwan
countries.residencyNote: Taiwan Gold Card available for qualifying professionals; APRC (permanent residency) achievable after sustained residency. Foreign-sourced income receives favorable treatment for new residents.

sources.name: Numbeo cost-of-living index (Taipei vs US average)
Taiwan National Health Insurance premium schedule
Taiwan Ministry of Finance tax guidance for foreign residents

caveats: Foreign-source income (including US retirement accounts) generally enjoys favorable treatment — modeled here as low effective rate, but jurisdiction-specific.
NHI is one of the cheapest comprehensive systems in the developed world.
Cross-strait political risk is not modeled — outside the scope of a FIRE calculator.

countries.name: Thailand
countries.residencyNote: Long-Term Resident (LTR) visa: $80k+ passive income or $1M assets, 10-year stay. Retirement (O-A/O-X) visa: 50+, $25k savings + $2k/mo income or $80k savings. Thailand Privilege (Elite) visa: $14k-$70k+ membership fees.

sources.name: Numbeo cost-of-living index (Bangkok vs US average)
Thailand Revenue Department (foreign income remittance rules)
Wikipedia: Visa policy of Thailand

caveats: 2024 rule change: foreign income remitted in any year (not just same year) is taxable for residents. Major reversal of previous loophole.
Chiang Mai and Hua Hin meaningfully cheaper than Bangkok. Phuket more expensive.
Private healthcare excellent in Bangkok/Phuket; rural areas limited.
Southern provinces near Malaysian border have insurgent activity — not relevant to expat retirement areas but reflected in national GPI.

countries.name: Malaysia
countries.residencyNote: Malaysia My Second Home (MM2H) program: tiered (Silver/Gold/Platinum) with fixed deposit requirements $150k-$1M and monthly income proof. 5-20 year renewable. English widely spoken; strong expat infrastructure in Penang and KL.

sources.name: Numbeo cost-of-living index (Kuala Lumpur vs US average)
Inland Revenue Board Malaysia (foreign-source income exemption)
Wikipedia: Malaysia My Second Home

caveats: Foreign-source income generally exempt from Malaysian tax for individuals; verify current rules with a cross-border specialist.
MM2H rules have been revised multiple times in recent years; always check current requirements.
Penang is a top-tier expat retirement destination with strong healthcare and English fluency.

countries.name: Vietnam
countries.residencyNote: No dedicated retirement visa. Most expats use 1- or 5-year tourist/business visa cycles or marriage-based residency. Investor visas available with capital commitment.

sources.name: Numbeo cost-of-living index (Hanoi vs US average)
General Department of Taxation Vietnam (resident worldwide income)
Wikipedia: Visa policy of Vietnam

caveats: Visa pathway is the dominant constraint, not the math. Vietnam has no straightforward retirement visa.
Private healthcare in HCMC and Hanoi is improving but international hospital fees can be high.
Hanoi and HCMC are major cities; Da Nang and Hoi An popular with expats at lower cost.
